title: Apple crumble pie
subtitle: Shortcrust pastry base with spiced apple and an oat crumble top
excerpt: A hybrid of apple pie and apple crumble: a shortcrust pastry base and sides hold a filling of lightly spiced, lemon-brightened apples, and the top is finished with a buttery oat crumble instead of a pastry lid. The crumble top gives a different texture contrast to a standard pie, and the open construction means the filling can be seen. Apples should be peeled and cut thickly so they hold their shape rather than cooking to a purée.
sourceNotes: Apple pie with a shortcrust base is documented in British and American cookery literature from at least the seventeenth century. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), gives apple pie recipes. The oat crumble topping as a pie finish is a variation appearing in British and American domestic baking guides from the early twentieth century, combining the crumble pudding tradition with the pie format.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked, assembled. Bake from frozen at 180°C, adding 15 minutes to the bake time and tenting with foil if the crumble colours too fast.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the pie and refrigerate unbaked for up to 24 hours. Bake from cold, allowing an extra 5 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the crumble topping is golden and the pastry is visibly cooked at the sides.

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm deep tart tin or pie dish.

glossaryTerms.definition: Peeling, coring, and slicing apples thickly (about 8 to 10 mm) rather than finely for a pie filling that holds its texture during baking rather than collapsing to purée. Toss the slices immediately with lemon juice to prevent browning. The amount of sugar depends on the variety: eating apples need less sugar than Bramley or other cooking apples. Resting the apples with the sugar for 10 minutes draws out some juice before the pie is assembled, which can be poured off to reduce sogginess in the finished base.

content.text: This pie has two layers of texture: the crisp shortcrust base and sides, and the buttery crumble on top. The apple filling in the middle should have some texture, not collapse to a sauce.
Cut the apples thickly and rest them briefly with the sugar
to draw off excess juice before filling the pie, which keeps the pastry base from getting soggy.

items.prepNote: peeled, cored, and sliced 8 to 10 mm thick

content.text: Make the pastry by rubbing the cold butter into the flour, sugar, and salt until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add the egg yolk and cold water and bring together into a dough. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Roll out and line a 23 cm tart tin, pressing into the sides. Return to the refrigerator while you prepare the filling.

Toss the apple slices with the sugar, cinnamon, and lemon juice. Leave for 10 minutes, then pour off any pooled juice. Make the crumble by rubbing the cold butter into the flour, oats, and dark brown sugar until it clumps in pea-sized pieces.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Fill the chilled pastry case with the apple slices, pressing down gently. Scatter the crumble evenly over the top. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the crumble is golden and the pastry is cooked at the sides. Cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing; the filling sets as it cools.
